text: The surgeon begins the procedure by excising adhesions to facilitate exploration of the diaphragm. The falciform ligament may be sectioned if necessary, followed by a full-thickness excision of endometriotic lesions.
The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament during the procedure, preparing to perform suturing using a grasp due to the unavailability of needle holders.
The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament while noting the unusual conformation of the patient's chest, which is positioned closely and may pose challenges during the surgical procedure.
The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament amidst numerous adhesions and considers cutting it to increase mobility, noting the close proximity of the chest and diaphragm.

The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament and proceeds to examine the peritoneum between the diaphragm and the liver. An endometriosis lesion containing chocolate fluid is visible, and the surgeon carefully uses suction to manage the fluid.

The surgeon gently dissects the severe diaphragmatic endometriosis, carefully navigating the movements of the diaphragm. The diaphragm's motion may require coordination with the anesthesiologist to temporarily halt ventilation for better access.
The surgeon encounters numerous adhesions in the surgical field, which are being carefully assessed and prepared for removal. The diaphragm is visible, indicating previous surgical intervention.

The surgeon examines the diaphragm, identifying both small and large lesions that need removal. The decision is made to potentially resect the diaphragm entirely, which may involve cutting the posterior ramification of the phrenic nerve. The hepatic vein is noted to be nearby but not in direct contact with the lesions.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the scissors in the working area, ensuring not to push the tip without visual confirmation of the underlying structures. The surgical field is prepared for further dissection.

The surgeon begins by removing small lesions without opening the diaphragm to avoid pneumothorax. The peritoneum is opened, revealing that the diaphragm is more thickened laterally and less thickened at the phrenic center. The surgeon starts the procedure laterally, where opening the diaphragm is less common.
The surgeon approaches the diaphragm fibers, preparing to resect small lesions visible on the surface before proceeding to open the diaphragm.
The surgical field shows the diaphragm with visible thickening. The surgeon is working outside the diaphragm, assessing the area with small lesions.
The surgeon is positioned laterally, using a trocar for the bipolar instrument, and observes the thickened peritoneum while approaching larger lesions.
The surgeon has resected most of the visible lesions without opening the diaphragm, ensuring the surgical field remains manageable and avoiding complications such as pneumothorax.
The surgeon inspects the diaphragm, identifying and addressing the last small lesion visible on its surface.

The surgeon attempts to remove lesions from the diaphragm, carefully working to minimize the size of the patch needed for closure. The diaphragm appears thin, indicating that opening it may become necessary.
The surgeon inspects the prepared diaphragm area, ensuring there is enough tissue wedge for suturing. The diaphragm is opened, revealing the lung beneath.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the robotic arms to improve the grasping and visualization with the endoscope, ensuring better maneuverability within the surgical field. The procedure continues smoothly without the need to stop ventilation.

The surgeon ties a knot to secure the first V-lock suture, ensuring it remains tight. The second V-lock suture is also secured. The surgical field is nearly complete, and attention is given to managing the carbopneumoperitoneum. The surgeon prepares to close the final knot while instructing Milen to aspirate inside the surgical area.
